name: Fine-Tuning vs Prompting Tradeoffs
description: The decision between adapting model behavior via prompting (few-shot, system prompt) vs. parameter-level adaptation (full fine-tuning, LoRA, QLoRA). Fine-tuning offers better performance on narrow distributions, lower inference latency (no few-shot examples in context), and style internalization. Prompting offers flexibility, no training cost, and easier iteration. RLHF and RLAIF add alignment fine-tuning to the mix.
contrarian_take: Most fine-tuning papers compare against weak few-shot baselines to show their technique wins — but well-engineered prompts with good examples close the gap more often than the papers suggest. The real question is not fine-tuning vs. prompting but whether the performance delta justifies the training pipeline maintenance cost and the brittleness to out-of-distribution inputs. Fine-tuning creates a debt that few teams account for.
example_signals: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
The False Promise of Fine-Tuning: When Prompting Matches Adapted Models
LIMA: Less Is More for Alignment
